corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: We use BEIR to compare diverse, recent, state-of-the-art retrieval architectures with a focus on transformer-based neural approaches.
We studied the effectiveness of ten different retrieval models and demonstrate, that in-domain performance cannot predict how well an approach will generalize in a zero-shot setup.

Our study on annotation selection bias highlights the challenge of evaluating new models on existing datasets: Even though TREC-COVID is based on the predictions from many systems, contributed by a diverse set of teams, we found largely different Hole@10 rates for the tested systems, negatively affecting non-lexical approaches.
The BEIR software provides an is an easy to use Python framework (pip install beir) for model evaluation.
For example, dense retrievers are observed to underperform on datasets with a large domain shift compared from what they have been trained on, like in BioASQ, or task-shifts like in Touché-2020.
To measure the diversity in domains, we compute the domain overlap between the pairwise datasets using a pairwise weighted Jaccard similarity score on unigram word overlap between all dataset pairs.

From Figure , we observe a rather low weighted Jaccard word overlap across different domains, indicating that BEIR is a challenging benchmark where approaches must generalize well to diverse out-of-distribution domains.
Depending upon the nature and requirements of real-world applications, retrieval tasks can be either be precision or recall focused.
Depending upon the nature and requirements of real-world applications, retrieval tasks can be either be precision or recall focused.
Decision support metrics such as Precision and Recall which are both rank unaware are not suitable.
Decision support metrics such as Precision and Recall which are both rank unaware are not suitable.
Binary rank-aware metrics such as MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rate) and MAP (Mean Average Precision) fail to evaluate tasks with graded relevance judgements.
Binary rank-aware metrics such as MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rate) and MAP (Mean Average Precision) fail to evaluate tasks with graded relevance judgements.

Besides the included models, the BEIR benchmark is model agnostic and in future different model configurations can be easily incorporated within the benchmark. (i) Lexical Retrieval: (a) BM25 is a commonly-used bag-of-words retrieval function based on token-matching between two high-dimensional sparse vectors with TF-IDF token weights.

We fine-tune a DistilBERT model on the MS MARCO dataset and use sparse-vectors with 2,000 non-zero entries. (c) DocT5query is a popular document expansion technique using a T5 (base) model trained on MS MARCO to generate synthetic queries and append them to the original document for lexical search.

The Multi-DPR model is a bert-base-uncased model trained on four QA datasets (including titles): NQ , TriviaQA , WebQuestions and CuratedTREC . (b) ANCE is a bi-encoder constructing hard negatives from an Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) index of the corpus, which in parallel updates to select hard negative training instances during fine-tuning of the model.
Then, for a target dataset we generate 5 queries for each document using a combination of top-k and nucleus-sampling (top-k: 25; top-p: 0.95).
Due to resource constraints, we cap the maximum number of target documents in each dataset to 100K.

We train a bert-base-uncased model, with maximum sequence length of 300 on the MS MARCO dataset for 300K steps. (v) Re-ranking model: (a) BM25 + CE reranks the top-100 retrieved hits from a first-stage BM25 (Anserini) model.
In this section, we evaluate and analyze how retrieval models perform on the BEIR benchmark.
Table reports the results of all evaluated systems on the selected benchmark datasets.
This stresses the point, that retrieval methods must be evaluated on a broad range of datasets.

The authors of 4 out of the 19 datasets in the BEIR benchmark (NFCorpus, FiQA-2018, Quora, Climate-Fever) do not report the dataset license in the paper or a repository; We overview the rest: • MSMARCO: Provided under "MIT License" for non-commercial research purposes. • FEVER, NQ, DBPedia, Signal-1M: All provided under CC BY-SA 3.0 license. • TREC-NEWS, Robust04, BioASQ: Data collection archives are under Copyright. • ArguAna, Touché-2020: Provided under CC BY 4.0 license. • CQADupStack: Provided under Apache License 2.0 license. • SciFact: Provided under the CC BY-NC 2.0 license. • SCIDOCS: Provided under the GNU General Public License v3.0 license. • HotpotQA: Provided under the CC BY-SA 4.0 license. • TREC-COVID: Provided under the "Dataset License Agreement".

The authors of 4 out of the 19 datasets in the BEIR benchmark (NFCorpus, FiQA-2018, Quora, Climate-Fever) do not report the dataset license in the paper or a repository; We overview the rest: • MSMARCO: Provided under "MIT License" for non-commercial research purposes. • FEVER, NQ, DBPedia, Signal-1M: All provided under CC BY-SA 3.0 license. • TREC-NEWS, Robust04, BioASQ: Data collection archives are under Copyright. • ArguAna, Touché-2020: Provided under CC BY 4.0 license. • CQADupStack: Provided under Apache License 2.0 license. • SciFact: Provided under the CC BY-NC 2.0 license. • SCIDOCS: Provided under the GNU General Public License v3.0 license. • HotpotQA: Provided under the CC BY-SA 4.0 license. • TREC-COVID: Provided under the "Dataset License Agreement".

To address this, and to facilitate researchers to broadly evaluate the effectiveness of their models, we introduce Benchmarking-IR (BEIR), a robust and heterogeneous evaluation benchmark for information retrieval.
